title: IT & Software
description: Essential vocabulary for software development and IT professionals

video.title: Software Development English for IT Professionals

vocabulary.meaning: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem
vocabulary.example: The sorting algorithm runs in O(n log n) time.

vocabulary.meaning: The process of finding and fixing errors in code
vocabulary.example: She spent hours debugging the authentication module.

vocabulary.meaning: Releasing software to a live environment
vocabulary.example: The deployment to production went smoothly.

vocabulary.meaning: A storage location for code managed by version control
vocabulary.example: Please clone the repository before making changes.

vocabulary.meaning: A request to merge code changes into a branch
vocabulary.example: Submit a pull request when your feature is ready.

vocabulary.meaning: A reusable set of libraries for building applications
vocabulary.example: We use React as our front-end framework.

vocabulary.meaning: Application Programming Interface ‚ÄĒ a way for programs to communicate
vocabulary.translation: giao diŠĽán lŠļ≠p tr√¨nh ŠĽ©ng dŠĽ•ng
vocabulary.example: The mobile app calls the REST API to fetch data.

vocabulary.meaning: An organized collection of structured data
vocabulary.example: We migrated from MySQL to PostgreSQL database.

vocabulary.meaning: A computer that provides services to other computers
vocabulary.example: The server handles thousands of requests per second.

vocabulary.meaning: Delivering computing services over the internet
vocabulary.example: We moved our infrastructure to cloud computing.

vocabulary.meaning: A software development methodology using iterative cycles
vocabulary.example: Our team follows agile development with two-week sprints.

vocabulary.meaning: A fixed time period to complete a set of tasks in agile
vocabulary.example: We plan our tasks at the beginning of each sprint.

vocabulary.meaning: Restructuring code without changing its behavior
vocabulary.example: Refactoring improved the codebase maintainability.

vocabulary.meaning: The ability of a system to handle increased load
vocabulary.example: Scalability is a key requirement for the new platform.

vocabulary.meaning: The delay between a request and response in a system
vocabulary.example: We need to reduce API latency below 100 milliseconds.

vocabulary.meaning: Converting data into a coded form to prevent unauthorized access
vocabulary.example: All passwords are stored with AES encryption.

vocabulary.meaning: Verifying the identity of a user or system
vocabulary.example: Two-factor authentication adds an extra security layer.

vocabulary.meaning: An architecture style splitting apps into small independent services
vocabulary.example: We broke the monolith into microservices last year.

vocabulary.meaning: A system that tracks changes to files over time
vocabulary.example: Git is the most popular version control system.

vocabulary.meaning: Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery ‚ÄĒ automated build and release
vocabulary.translation: t√≠ch hŠĽ£p v√† ph√Ęn phŠĽĎi li√™n tŠĽ•c
vocabulary.example: Our CI/CD pipeline runs tests on every commit.

vocabulary.meaning: Packaging software with its dependencies into containers
vocabulary.example: Docker enables containerization of our services.

vocabulary.meaning: A device that distributes network traffic across servers
vocabulary.example: The load balancer routes requests to healthy servers.

vocabulary.meaning: Software with publicly available source code
vocabulary.example: Linux is a famous open source operating system.

vocabulary.meaning: Software that connects different applications or services
vocabulary.example: The middleware handles authentication between services.

vocabulary.meaning: The maximum data transfer rate of a network connection
vocabulary.example: Video streaming requires high bandwidth.

vocabulary.meaning: Temporary storage for frequently accessed data
vocabulary.example: We use Redis as a cache layer to speed up queries.

vocabulary.meaning: A function that calls itself to solve sub-problems
vocabulary.example: Tree traversal is often implemented with recursion.

vocabulary.meaning: A contract defining how components interact
vocabulary.example: All payment providers implement the same interface.

vocabulary.meaning: A class deriving properties and methods from a parent class
vocabulary.example: Inheritance reduces code duplication.

vocabulary.meaning: The ability of objects to take many forms
vocabulary.example: Polymorphism allows flexible and extensible code design.

vocabulary.meaning: Providing dependencies to a class from outside
vocabulary.example: Dependency injection makes unit testing much easier.

vocabulary.meaning: Testing individual components of code in isolation
vocabulary.example: Every function should have at least one unit test.

vocabulary.meaning: Testing how multiple components work together
vocabulary.example: Integration tests verify the API endpoint responses.

vocabulary.meaning: The cost of shortcuts taken during development
vocabulary.example: We need to pay down our technical debt this quarter.

vocabulary.meaning: A prioritized list of features and tasks
vocabulary.example: The product backlog has 50 items to implement.

vocabulary.meaning: A person with interest or investment in a project
vocabulary.example: All stakeholders approved the new feature roadmap.

vocabulary.meaning: An isolated test environment safe for experimentation
vocabulary.example: Test the payment flow in the sandbox environment first.

vocabulary.meaning: A specific URL where an API receives requests
vocabulary.example: The /users endpoint returns all registered users.

vocabulary.meaning: A request for data from a database
vocabulary.example: This SQL query retrieves orders from the last 30 days.

vocabulary.meaning: A string used for authentication or session management
vocabulary.example: The JWT token expires after 24 hours.

vocabulary.meaning: An automated message sent from one app to another on an event
vocabulary.example: The payment gateway sends a webhook on successful charge.

vocabulary.meaning: A bug reintroduced after previously being fixed
vocabulary.example: The update caused a regression in the login flow.

vocabulary.meaning: An early sample or model built to test a concept
vocabulary.example: We built a prototype in two days to validate the idea.

vocabulary.meaning: Written descriptions of how software works
vocabulary.example: Good documentation helps new developers onboard faster.

vocabulary.meaning: Systematic examination of source code by peers
vocabulary.example: All changes require code review before merging.

vocabulary.meaning: An urgent patch applied to fix a critical bug in production
vocabulary.example: We deployed a hotfix to fix the login crash.

vocabulary.meaning: The time a system has been running without failure
vocabulary.example: We guarantee 99.9% uptime in our SLA.

vocabulary.meaning: The amount of data processed by a system per unit time
vocabulary.example: The new queue system doubled our throughput.

vocabulary.meaning: Standard reusable code with little customization needed
vocabulary.example: This template reduces the boilerplate for new projects.

vocabulary.meaning: The rules that define the structure of a programming language
vocabulary.example: Python syntax is clean and easy to read.

vocabulary.meaning: The period when a program is executing
vocabulary.example: A runtime error crashed the application in production.

vocabulary.meaning: A program that translates source code into machine code
vocabulary.example: The compiler detected three errors in the code.

vocabulary.meaning: A program that executes code line by line without compiling
vocabulary.example: Python uses an interpreter to run scripts.

vocabulary.meaning: An error caused by too many nested function calls
vocabulary.example: The recursive function caused a stack overflow error.

vocabulary.meaning: Hiding complex details and showing only essential features
vocabulary.example: Abstraction helps simplify complex systems.

vocabulary.meaning: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access
vocabulary.example: Encapsulation protects internal state from outside modification.

vocabulary.meaning: Practices combining software development and IT operations
vocabulary.example: Our DevOps team automates deployments and monitoring.

vocabulary.meaning: Automated coordination of multiple services or containers
vocabulary.example: Kubernetes handles container orchestration in our cluster.

vocabulary.meaning: A single large application containing all functionality
vocabulary.example: We are breaking the monolith into smaller services.

vocabulary.meaning: An operation that produces the same result no matter how many times it runs
vocabulary.example: PUT requests should be idempotent in REST APIs.

vocabulary.meaning: Marking a feature as outdated and scheduled for removal
vocabulary.example: The library issued a deprecation warning for that method.

vocabulary.meaning: Converting an object into a format suitable for storage or transmission
vocabulary.example: JSON serialization is common for API responses.

vocabulary.meaning: Analyzing code to detect potential errors and style issues
vocabulary.translation: kiŠĽÉm tra m√£ nguŠĽďn tŠĽĪ ńĎŠĽông
vocabulary.example: Enable linting in your editor to catch issues early.

vocabulary.meaning: The structure or blueprint of a database or data format
vocabulary.example: We updated the database schema to add a new column.

vocabulary.meaning: Moving data or systems from one environment to another
vocabulary.example: The database migration script ran without errors.

vocabulary.meaning: The data sent in the body of an API request or response
vocabulary.example: The POST request payload includes the user's name and email.

vocabulary.meaning: Controlling the number of requests a user can make in a time period
vocabulary.translation: giŠĽõi hŠļ°n tŠĽĎc ńĎŠĽô truy cŠļ≠p
vocabulary.example: Rate limiting prevents abuse of our public API.

vocabulary.meaning: Reverting a system to a previous working state
vocabulary.example: We performed a rollback after the failed deployment.

vocabulary.meaning: A backup system ready to take over if the primary fails
vocabulary.example: The standby database takes over during failover.

vocabulary.meaning: Setting up and configuring IT infrastructure for use
vocabulary.translation: cung cŠļ•p / thiŠļŅt lŠļ≠p hŠļ° tŠļßng
vocabulary.example: Server provisioning is now fully automated.

vocabulary.meaning: To restructure existing code without changing its external behavior
vocabulary.example: Let's refactor this function to improve readability.

vocabulary.meaning: To save changes to a version control repository
vocabulary.example: Remember to commit your code before the end of the day.

vocabulary.meaning: A separate line of development in version control
vocabulary.example: Create a new branch for each feature you work on.

vocabulary.meaning: When two changes to the same code cannot be automatically combined
vocabulary.example: We resolved the merge conflict before merging the branch.

vocabulary.meaning: An indicator in code that suggests a deeper problem
vocabulary.example: Long methods are a common code smell.

vocabulary.meaning: Two developers working together on the same code
vocabulary.example: Pair programming helps share knowledge across the team.

vocabulary.meaning: A meeting at the end of a sprint to demonstrate completed work
vocabulary.example: We demo the new features during the sprint review.

vocabulary.meaning: A meeting to reflect on what went well and what to improve
vocabulary.translation: buŠĽēi nh√¨n lŠļ°i / r√ļt kinh nghiŠĽám
vocabulary.example: The retrospective helped us improve our process.

vocabulary.meaning: A short description of a feature from the user's perspective
vocabulary.example: Each user story describes what the user wants to achieve.

vocabulary.meaning: Service Level Agreement ‚ÄĒ a contract defining expected service quality
vocabulary.translation: cam kŠļŅt chŠļ•t l∆įŠĽ£ng dŠĽčch vŠĽ•
vocabulary.example: Our SLA guarantees 99.9% availability.

vocabulary.meaning: The combination of technologies used to build an application
vocabulary.example: Our tech stack includes React, Node.js, and PostgreSQL.

vocabulary.meaning: Minimum Viable Product ‚ÄĒ the simplest version of a product that works
vocabulary.translation: sŠļ£n phŠļ©m khŠļ£ dŠĽ•ng tŠĽĎi thiŠĽÉu
vocabulary.example: We launched the MVP to get early user feedback.

vocabulary.meaning: Recording events and errors that occur in a running application
vocabulary.example: Proper logging makes it easier to diagnose production issues.

vocabulary.meaning: Continuously observing a system's health and performance
vocabulary.example: Our monitoring dashboard shows real-time server metrics.

vocabulary.meaning: Automatic switching to a backup system when the primary fails
vocabulary.example: Failover ensures zero downtime during server failures.

vocabulary.meaning: Operations that run independently without waiting for each other
vocabulary.example: Asynchronous calls prevent the UI from freezing.

vocabulary.meaning: A single small, independent service within a larger system
vocabulary.example: Each microservice handles a specific business function.

vocabulary.meaning: The ability to understand a system's internal state from its outputs
vocabulary.example: Good observability requires metrics, logs, and traces.

vocabulary.meaning: Releasing changes to a small group of users before full rollout
vocabulary.example: We used a canary deployment to test the new feature safely.

vocabulary.meaning: A toggle that enables or disables a feature without deploying new code
vocabulary.example: Use a feature flag to control the rollout of the new UI.

vocabulary.meaning: A single repository storing code for multiple projects
vocabulary.example: The team uses a monorepo for all frontend packages.

vocabulary.meaning: Packaging an application with all its dependencies into a container
vocabulary.translation: ńĎ√≥ng g√≥i ŠĽ©ng dŠĽ•ng trong container
vocabulary.example: Docker enables containerization of any application.

vocabulary.meaning: Automated coordination and management of containers or services
vocabulary.translation: ńĎiŠĽĀu phŠĽĎi dŠĽčch vŠĽ• tŠĽĪ ńĎŠĽông
vocabulary.example: Kubernetes handles orchestration of our microservices.

vocabulary.meaning: An operation that produces the same result no matter how many times it is applied
vocabulary.translation: t√≠nh bŠļ•t biŠļŅn khi lŠļ∑p lŠļ°i
vocabulary.example: HTTP GET requests should be idempotent.

vocabulary.meaning: A server that forwards client requests to backend servers
vocabulary.example: NGINX is commonly used as a reverse proxy.

vocabulary.meaning: A device that distributes traffic across multiple servers
vocabulary.example: The load balancer prevents any single server from being overwhelmed.

vocabulary.meaning: A data storage layer that temporarily stores frequently accessed data
vocabulary.example: Redis serves as a caching layer in our architecture.

vocabulary.meaning: An HTTP callback triggered automatically when an event occurs
vocabulary.translation: webhook - gŠĽći tŠĽĪ ńĎŠĽông khi c√≥ sŠĽĪ kiŠĽán
vocabulary.example: Set up a webhook to notify the app when a payment succeeds.

vocabulary.meaning: Software Development Kit ‚ÄĒ tools and libraries for building applications
vocabulary.translation: bŠĽô c√īng cŠĽ• ph√°t triŠĽÉn phŠļßn mŠĽĀm (SDK)
vocabulary.example: Download the mobile SDK to integrate our payment API.

vocabulary.meaning: A programming paradigm where actions are triggered by events
vocabulary.example: The system uses an event-driven architecture for real-time updates.

quiz.question: What is an algorithm?
options: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem

quiz.question: What is a pull request?
options: A request to merge code changes into a branch

quiz.question: What is debugging?
options: Finding and fixing errors in code

quiz.question: What is a cache?
options: Temporary storage for frequently accessed data

quiz.question: What does 'scalability' mean in IT?
options: The ability to write clean code
The ability of a system to handle increased load

quiz.question: What is a sprint in agile development?
options: A fixed time period to complete tasks

quiz.question: What is technical debt?
options: The cost of shortcuts taken during development

quiz.question: What is a webhook?
options: A type of fish hook in programming
An automated message sent between apps on an event

quiz.question: What is a 'rollback'?
options: Reverting a system to a previous working state

quiz.question: What does 'idempotent' mean?
options: Producing the same result no matter how many times it runs

quiz.question: What is a 'merge conflict'?
options: When two changes to the same code cannot be automatically combined

quiz.question: What is a 'feature flag'?
options: A toggle that enables or disables a feature without new code
